# The AI Design Tells — A Research Reference

A comprehensive, cited catalog of the patterns that reveal an interface, website, or graphic was
**generated by an LLM** rather than designed by a person. For product designers and frontend engineers
who want to recognize the generic "AI look" and deliberately avoid it.

This is the broad, general-purpose reference. For the project-specific version with this repo's
before/after diffs and a copy-paste detection script, see [`anti-ai-slop-design-guide.md`](./anti-ai-slop-design-guide.md).

*Compiled 2026-05-31 from a multi-source research pass (search → fetch → adversarial verification).
Sources listed at the end; key claims are linked inline.*

---

## The root cause — why every model converges on the same look

A model has no taste. Taste is lived experience compressed into an internal sense of "good" that's
easy to recognize and hard to articulate. An LLM only has **statistical correlations between tokens**,
so when asked for "a modern website" it reproduces the *statistical median* of its training data and
presents it as the best answer. The result is convergence: thousands of people prompting similar tools
get thousands of near-identical designs.

Three compounding mechanisms explain the specifics:

1. **A pragmatic default became a global standard.** Adam Wathan, the creator of Tailwind CSS, has
   [acknowledged in a semi-apologetic tweet](https://x.com/adamwathan/status/1953510802159219096) that
   he picked `indigo` as the accent color for Tailwind UI's example components around 2020 — *"not
   because it was the best color for buttons… it was just a neutral, inoffensive placeholder."*
   ([dev.to/alanwest](https://dev.to/alanwest/why-every-ai-built-website-looks-the-same-blame-tailwinds-indigo-500-3h2p))
2. **Training-corpus over-representation.** Models learned from billions of tokens of web code scraped
   roughly 2019–2024, in which `bg-indigo-500` appears disproportionately often. The model encodes
   "purple buttons are what web buttons look like" as fact, not preference.
3. **A feedback loop.** As AI-generated sites flood the web and become training data, the distribution
   shifts *further* toward the defaults — AI trained on AI output, amplifying the monoculture.

The same dynamic produces the canonical AI output, per the dev.to analysis: *Inter or system-ui font,
rounded corners on everything (`border-radius: 0.5rem`), subtle low-opacity box shadows, a three-column
feature grid with icons, and a hero section with gradient text.* If that list describes your page, a
model probably designed it.

> The fix is never "a better prompt." It is a **point of view** — one confident, specific decision the
> machine wouldn't average its way into. An ugly page with an opinion is more memorable than a polished
> page with none.

---

## 1. Color

| Tell | Specifics | Why it reads as AI | The human fix |
|---|---|---|---|
| **The purple/indigo/violet default** | Tailwind `indigo-500` `#6366F1`; violet `#7C3AED`, `#8B5CF6`; "VibeCode purple" lavender accents | The single strongest signal — the Tailwind placeholder cargo-culted across the corpus ([communeify](https://www.communeify.com/en/blog/why-ai-loves-purple-global-design-convergence/), [gradientshub](https://gradientshub.com/blog/why-does-ai-have-and-indigo-obsession-in-web-design/)) | Choose **one** confident, on-domain hue and commit. Never accept the default purple. |
| **Blue → purple gradients** | `from-blue-500 to-purple-600`, diagonal hero washes, gradient CTAs | "The official color scheme of *we used AI*" ([Kai Ni](https://medium.com/@kai.ni/design-observation-why-do-ai-generated-websites-always-favour-blue-purple-gradients-ea91bf038d4c), [prg.sh](https://prg.sh/ramblings/Why-Your-AI-Keeps-Building-the-Same-Purple-Gradient-Website)) | Flat brand color, or a restrained tonal gradient inside the brand hue. Gradients earn their place; they aren't the baseline. |
| **The teal/cyan "secondary"** | `#06B6D4`, `#14B8A6` paired with the indigo as the second accent | The other half of the default palette pairing | A secondary that's analogous or a true complement chosen on purpose, not the stock cyan. |
| **Timid, low-saturation palettes** | Everything desaturated and "safe," nothing committed | The model averages toward inoffensive | Commit to contrast and saturation where it matters. Safe = invisible. |
| **Dark mode + purple glow** | Near-black bg with a violet radial glow / neon accent | The default "modern/premium" reach when no theme is specified | If dark, light it with intent; drop the ambient glow entirely. |

Roots of the bias are documented in depth at [deeplearning.fr](https://deeplearning.fr/the-hidden-purple-bias-in-ai-generated-interfaces-uncovering-the-technical-roots-and-building-better-prompts/)
and [jackpearce.co.uk](https://www.jackpearce.co.uk/notes/purple-gradient-ai-aesthetics/).

## 2. Typography

| Tell | Specifics | Why it reads as AI | The human fix |
|---|---|---|---|
| **The Inter monoculture** | `Inter` (or `system-ui`) for *everything*, body and display | Inter is the default font in nearly every AI tool, component library, and builder — an aesthetic monoculture ([925studios](https://www.925studios.co/blog/ai-slop-web-design-guide), [developersdigest](https://www.developersdigest.tech/blog/ai-design-slop-and-how-to-spot-it)) | Pair a distinct **display** face with the body font. Body Inter is fine; *Inter-for-everything* is the tell. |
| **The other "safe" defaults** | `Poppins`, `Montserrat`, `Roboto`, `DM Sans`, `Plus Jakarta Sans`, `Space Grotesk`, `Geist` | Geometric/grotesque Google-font staples the model reaches for; even the "alternatives" have become defaults ([Typewolf](https://www.typewolf.com/google-fonts), [Figma](https://www.figma.com/resource-library/best-fonts-for-websites/)) | A face with actual character chosen for the brand. If you use one of these, *pair* and *tune* it; don't ship it raw. |
| **Gradient text** | `bg-clip-text` rainbow/indigo headline | A signature hero move of generated pages (dev.to root-cause list) | Solid headline color. Let the words carry weight, not a gradient. |
| **All-caps eyebrow labels** | `LETTER-SPACED · UPPERCASE · MICRO LABEL` above every section title | Default section-header pattern, used reflexively | Sentence case; reserve small-caps for places it earns (table heads). |
| **Oversized hero type with no hierarchy** | Giant `text-7xl` headline, everything else uniform | Scale without a system | A real modular scale; hierarchy from size **+ weight + color**. |
| **Weight soup** | 5+ weights (300/400/500/700/800) mixed with no logic | No weight system | Cap at three: 400 body, 500 labels, 600 headings. |

## 3. Layout & structure

| Tell | Why it reads as AI | The human fix |
|---|---|---|
| **Centered hero + pill badge above the H1** (`◦ New: …` chip, centered headline, two CTAs) | The single most-copied landing layout in the corpus | Vary the hero — asymmetry, a real product screenshot, a data-first header. |
| **Bento grids** | The 2023–24 trend the model freezes on; rounded tiles of mismatched sizes | Use a grid because the content needs it, not for decoration. |
| **Three-icon feature card row** | The "three boxes with icons" tutorial grid, nearly always ×3 | Differentiate cards by content weight; commit to one layout, not three identical variants. |
| **Numbered 1-2-3 step sequences** | Filler structure that conveys no real process | Use markers tied to the actual domain/flow. |
| **Stat-banner rows** ("10k+ users · 99.9% uptime · 24/7") | Generic credibility filler | Integrate real, specific numbers into the narrative. |
| **Identical section rhythm** | Every section the same padding, the same card, the same shape | Vary density and treatment so the page has a pulse. |
| **`max-w-7xl mx-auto` everywhere** | The default container, untouched | Choose measure per content type; full-bleed where it serves. |

Catalogued across [developersdigest](https://www.developersdigest.tech/blog/ai-design-slop-and-how-to-spot-it),
[925studios](https://www.925studios.co/blog/ai-slop-web-design-guide), and
[dev.to: why every AI landing page looks the same](https://dev.to/_46ea277e677b888e0cd13/why-every-ai-generated-landing-page-looks-the-same-and-how-to-fix-it-1kmo).

## 4. Surface & effects

| Tell | Specifics | Why it reads as AI | The human fix |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Glassmorphism everywhere** | `backdrop-blur` frosted cards floating in soft pastel voids | A trend the model never moved past | Solid tonal surfaces. A frosted *topbar* is fine — that's chrome, not content. |
| **Soft shadow on every card + colored glows** | low-opacity `shadow-lg/xl`, neon box-shadows, radial accent glows | Decoration without function; "floating cards in a void" | Shadow = elevation, only on genuinely floating layers (menu/dialog/popover). Surfaces flat with hairline borders. |
| **Uniform radius / pill everything** | every element `rounded-2xl` or `rounded-full`; the default `0.5rem` on all | One slider dragged across the whole UI | Geometry is a per-element decision. Crisp buttons, pill *chips*, a notch softer on cards. |
| **Colored single-side accent stripe** | 3–4px brand bar on the left/top of cards, nav items, alerts | The component-library-demo tell — decoration posing as an indicator | Carry state/category with a **solid tonal tint fill** or the text/icon color. No stripe. |
| **Gradient borders / neon outlines** | `border-image` rainbow rings, glowing focus halos | "Futuristic" default with no purpose | Hairline borders; a single accent on focus, AA-visible. |

## 5. Iconography & imagery

| Tell | Why it reads as AI | The human fix |
|---|---|---|
| **Emoji as icons** | The fastest "vibe-coded" tell — 🚀✨ as section/nav glyphs | One real icon set, used consistently. |
| **The default icon sets, untouched** | `Lucide` / `Heroicons` everywhere (the shadcn/Tailwind pairing) | Fine to use — but the *same set + same purple + same Inter* together is the giveaway. Vary stroke, size, and intent. |
| **Corporate Memphis / "Alegria"** | Flat, big-limbed, faceless figures in bright flat colors — the 2017–2021 Big Tech house style ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Corporate_Memphis), [Webflow](https://webflow.com/blog/corporate-memphis), [Creative Bloq: "it's dead"](https://www.creativebloq.com/news/corporate-memphis-style-is-dead)) | Was a designer trend; now a generated default that signals "soulless tech." Use real, specific illustration or none. |
| **Plastic AI illustrations / 3D blobs** | Overly smooth, symmetrical, glossy renders and gradient blobs | The generative-image default look | Real product UI, real screenshots, or commissioned art that matches the brand. |
| **Stock "diverse team at a laptop"** | The generic well-lit-office photo | Communicates nothing about the product | Real team photos, real artifacts, real screenshots. |

## 6. Copy & microcopy

The verbal counterpart of the visual slop. The flagged vocabulary (see "ChatGPT words to avoid"
catalogs such as [walterwrites.ai](https://walterwrites.ai/most-common-chatgpt-words-to-avoid/)):

- **Verbs:** *supercharge, unleash, elevate, harness, leverage, delve, dive in, streamline, empower, unlock.*
- **Adverbs/adjectives:** *seamlessly, effortlessly, robust, cutting-edge, best-in-class, world-class, game-changing, delightful.*
- **Phrases:** *"all-in-one platform," "build the future of X," "in today's fast-paced world," "take it to the next level," "the intersection of X and Y."*
- **Constructions:** the "**It's not just X — it's Y**" reversal; the rule-of-three tricolon ("faster, smarter, simpler"); rhetorical "Whether you're … or …, we've got you covered."
- **Em-dash overuse** — widely cited as a tell, but **contested**: em-dashes are also a hallmark of good human writing, and OpenAI added a setting to suppress them, so it is a weak signal on its own ([Rolling Stone: "ChatGPT Hyphen"](https://www.rollingstone.com/culture/culture-features/chatgpt-hypen-em-dash-ai-writing-1235314945/)). Treat as corroborating, not conclusive.

**Fix:** write in a real voice with specifics. "Financial infrastructure for the internet" beats "the
all-in-one platform to supercharge your business." Concrete claims, the founder's actual words, numbers
that are true.

## 7. Motion

| Tell | Why it reads as AI | The human fix |
|---|---|---|
| **Fade-in-on-scroll on everything** | The one animation generated pages ship, applied to every element | Animate for state change, attention, or brand — not as default decoration. |
| **Missing hover/focus states** | The model styles the resting state and forgets the rest | Design hover, active, focus, disabled, loading as first-class states. |
| **Snap, no easing** | Buttons jump instead of ease; no transition tokens | Purposeful micro-interactions with real easing curves and durations. |

(Motion-quality guidance: [LottieFiles motion-design skill](https://github.com/LottieFiles/motion-design-skill).)

## 8. Accessibility — the invisible tell

Generated UIs are frequently **inaccessible by default**, because contrast and focus aren't in the
"looks modern" objective the model optimizes for ([Frontend Masters: "AI-generated UI is inaccessible by
default"](https://frontendmasters.com/blog/ai-generated-ui-is-inaccessible-by-default/)).

- **Low-contrast text** — medium-grey body on white/off-white, failing WCAG AA (4.5:1). The "timid
  palette" tell with real consequences.
- **Removed/invisible focus indicators** — `outline: none` with nothing replacing it ([focus-indicator
  guide](https://www.accessguide.io/guide/focus-indicator)).
- **Color-only status** — meaning carried by hue alone, no text/icon.

**Fix:** body text ≥ 4.5:1, large text ≥ 3:1; a visible AA focus ring on every interactive element;
never encode meaning in color alone. Accessibility is part of "looks professional."

---

## Quick reference — the tell in one line each

1. Purple/indigo primary or blue→purple gradient → pick one committed, on-domain hue.
2. Inter (or Poppins/Montserrat/Space Grotesk) for everything → pair a real display face.
3. Gradient text headline → solid color.
4. Centered hero + pill badge over the H1 → vary the hero.
5. Bento grid / three-icon cards / 1-2-3 steps → structure from content, not template.
6. Glassmorphism + soft shadows + glows on content → flat tonal surfaces, hairline borders.
7. Uniform `rounded-2xl` / pill everything → geometry per element.
8. Colored single-side accent stripe → tonal tint fill instead.
9. Emoji icons / default Lucide+purple+Inter combo → one tuned icon system.
10. Corporate Memphis / plastic 3D / stock office photos → real, specific imagery.
11. "Supercharge / seamlessly / all-in-one" copy → specific, true, in your voice.
12. Fade-in-on-scroll, no hover states, no easing → purposeful motion + full state coverage.
13. Low contrast, no focus rings → WCAG AA, visible focus, never color-only.

The meta-test: **could a stranger describe your product's point of view in one sentence after five
seconds?** If every choice is the median, the answer is no — and that is the actual problem AI slop names.

---
